segments.s269-g97: که introduces reported speech ('that'); گفتی is the 2nd-person past ‘you said’ (classical usage). ز بهر is a fixed archaic combination meaning 'for/because of' or 'on account of'; تنی گشته خاک literally 'a body that has become dust' (گشته is a perfect participle 'has become', modifying تن). The whole segment sets up the reason/clause for what follows.
segments.s269-g98: نشاید is an impersonal archaic modal meaning 'it is not fitting/ought not'; it governs the following verb ستد, here functioning as an infinitival/present form 'to take' (classical usage). زنده را marks 'the living' with the object particle را, and جان پاک ('pure life') is the direct object phrase: 'to take the pure life from the living.' Word order places the modal + verb before the object phrase.
segments.s269-g99: تو بشناس is an imperative (or hortative) 'you, know/recognize'. The following noun phrase کین زشت کردارها ('these hateful, vile deeds') is the content to be known; کین qualifies کردارها as deeds motivated by hatred or vengeance, while زشت gives the moral sense 'vile'.
segments.s269-g100: به دل پر ز is an idiomatic ordering: به دل ('in/to the heart') + پر ز ('full of') + هر گونه آزارها ('every kind of harms/sufferings'). پر functions as an adjective 'full (of)', followed by ز (from/of) plus the plural آزارها. The phrase describes the heart being filled with all sorts of torment.
segments.s269-g101: که again introduces the explanatory clause. Word order is topical: با شهر ایران ('to/against the city of Iran') precedes the pronoun شما ('you, pl.'), and کرده‌اید is the present-perfect (compound past) 'you have done' (pl.). با here marks the target/recipient of their actions ('toward the city of Iran').
segments.s269-g102: چه مایه is an exclamatory phrase 'what amount/what extent'; کیان را ('the kings') is marked by the object particle را; بیازرده‌اید is a classical perfect form meaning 'you have hurt/grieved/afflicted' (here active, plural you). The segment asks, rhetorically, how severely the royal house has been harmed by them.
segments.s269-g103: The repeated چه serves to coordinate/contrast actions ('چه ... چه ...' = 'whether ... or ...' / 'be it ... or ...'). پیمان شکستن and کین ساختن are infinitival/nominal verb forms ('breaking covenants' and 'creating/harboring enmity') presented as alternates or joint vices.
segments.s269-g104: همیشه (always) modifies the verbal noun/infinitive تاختن ('to charge/rush'); به سوی is a locative/directional compound 'toward', and بدی is the abstract noun 'evil/harm'. The whole phrase describes a habitual action: 'always rushing toward evil.'
segments.s269-g33: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). داد is a noun meaning 'justice' and بودی is the past-tense form of بودن used here in a subjunctive/conditional sense ('had been' / 'were'); the phrase به دلت اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/within your heart' where دل takes the 2nd-person enclitic -ت and اندرون follows as an adverb 'inside'. The overall clause = 'if there were justice within your heart'.
segments.s269-g34: ترا + را mark the person affected (object/emphatic dative 'you'); پیشدستی is a noun 'precedence, initiative' and نبودی is the negative past form of بودن ('there would not have been' / 'you would not have had'). به خون qualifies the kind of precedence ('in blood' = in the matter of bloodshed). The clause says that you would not have had the first-mover advantage in bloodshed.
segments.s269-g35: کز is the archaic/preclassical preposition 'from' here; آغاز کار is the noun phrase 'the beginning of the affair', and اندر آمد is a verb phrase 'came in / entered' with the adverb نخست 'first' after it for emphasis. Word order is literary: 'from the beginning of the matter there came first...' (i.e. 'at the outset').
segments.s269-g36: نبودی is the past negative of بودن ('there was not'). به خون ریختن is a verbal-noun/infinitive phrase 'the shedding/spilling of blood', and هیچ سست is 'any weakness' (هیچ = none/any; سست = weak/lax). Put together: 'there was no weakness in shedding blood' — i.e. no hesitation about bloodshed.
segments.s269-g37: نخستین is 'the first' and that is followed by a relative clause beginning with که ('who/that') — که آمد به پیش تو = 'who came before you'. The verb آمد is simple past 'came', به پیش تو = 'to your presence / before you', and گیو (the name) appears after the clause as the noun being described.
segments.s269-g38: از ایران is 'from Iran' (از = from). هشیوار modifies مردان as an adjective 'alert/awake', and مردان here means 'men' or 'heroes'; نیو is an epithet following the noun (a poetic/archaic adjective). The phrase characterizes Giv as an alert man of Iran with the epithet نیو.
segments.s269-g39: بسازیده is a participial/poetic form meaning 'having made/formed' or 'made', followed by مر ('to/for me' — archaic dative) and then the object جنگ را 'the war' marked by را; لشکری is the noun 'an army' or 'troops' that is the result or object of that making. The clause means 'he prepared for me an army for the war.'
segments.s269-g40: ز is the poetic form of 'from'; کشور دمان = 'the border/hem of the country' with دمان meaning 'skirt/border'. تا marks the extent 'as far as/to', and دگر کشوری = 'another country/realm' (دگر = other, کشوری = pertaining to a country). Together: 'from the country's borders as far as another land.'
segments.s269-g9: دلت is دل + the 2nd-person suffix -ت = “your heart.” The prepositional phrase با زبان (“with the tongue/speech”) follows the possessor and the clause ends with هیچ همسایه نیست, a negative existential phrase meaning “is no neighbor/does not accord.” Together it says literally “your heart is no neighbor with your speech,” i.e. your heart does not match your words.
segments.s269-g10: روان ترا is روان + the 2nd-person suffix -ت plus the object/topic marker را (here emphasizing “your soul/spirit”). The predicate از خرد مایه نیست literally “has no portion of wisdom” (مایه = share/resource). نیست is the general present negative copula: “is not / has not.”
segments.s269-g11: به هرجای is an adverbial phrase “to/at whatever place” (wherever). چربی is the thing used (“fat”), and به کار آوری is the verb phrase (آوری, 2nd-person present/imperative form here) meaning “you put to work / you use.” Word order places the locative/adverbial up front: “wherever you put fat to use.”
segments.s269-g12: چنین is an adverb “thus/so,” followed by the subject تو. سخن پرنگار is the object phrase “ornamented/ richly pictured speech” (پرنگار = full of ornament). The verb آوری repeats the same verb as the previous clause, ‘you produce/bring forth,’ forming a paired stylistic statement.
segments.s269-g13: کسی را marks “someone” (را as object/topic marker) and is qualified by the relative clause که از بن نباشد خرد — “who does not have wisdom from the root / at the core.” که introduces the relative clause; از بن modifies نباشد (negated copula) to indicate lack at the foundation.
segments.s269-g14: گمان ... برد is a verbal idiom meaning “forms the opinion / supposes.” The phrase گمان بر تو (literally “the thought toward you”) and بر مهربانی (“toward kindness/that you are kind”) together specify what is supposed. برد here is the verbal head “takes/forms” (classical present/past sense: ‘he/she/one takes it to be’).
segments.s269-g15: چو introduces a simile/comparison “like/as.” شوره زمینی = “a saline/salt plain (earth).” که از دور آب is a relative clause “that from afar (از دور) [seems] water” with آب placed at the end for poetic effect; the verb showing/appearing is delayed to the next segment (نماید).
segments.s269-g16: نماید is an impersonal/third-person verb “it shows / it appears.” The subordinate temporal clause چو تابد بر او آفتاب (“when the sun shines upon it”) follows, with a poetic inversion (چو تابد بر او آفتاب instead of آفتاب چو بر او تابد). Together: “it appears [to show water] when the sun shines on it.”
segments.s269-g201: نخستین is an adverb/ordinal placed up front meaning “first.” به introduces the target of the forthcoming action (here: the mass of wounded). انبوه زخمی is a noun phrase in which انبوه (a crowd/host) is qualified by زخمی (wounded), and چو کوه is a postposed simile — “like a mountain” — describing that mass.
segments.s269-g202: بباید + infinitive (زدن) expresses necessity: “it is necessary to strike.” زدَن is the infinitive “to strike.” سر بسر is an adverbial phrase meaning literally “head by head / one by one” (here indicating successive blows); همگروه refers to the grouped opponents or fellows in the formation — i.e. those to be struck. Word order puts the modality first (بباید) followed by the verbal infinitive and the adverbial qualifier.
segments.s269-g203: میان دو لشکر is a prepositional phrase “between the two hosts/armies.” دو صف is the direct object “two lines/ranks.” برکشید is an imperative or jussive form of برکشیدن meaning “raise/draw up” (here: form the battle lines). The verb follows the prepositional phrase; the numerals modify the noun they precede.
segments.s269-g204: گر introduces a conditional “if.” ایدونک is a particle meaning roughly “in that way / thus” (emphatic/colloquial in classical diction). پیروزی is the subject “victory,” and آید پدید is a two-word verbal expression meaning “come into view / appear” (آید = comes; پدید = appears/manifests). Word order is conditional phrase → result clause.
segments.s269-g205: وگرنه = “otherwise.” همین here is an emphasizer meaning “these very / these same.” نامداران مرد is a classical-styled noun phrase: نامداران “the renowned ones” qualified by مرد in the sense “men/warriors/heroes.” The phrase nominates the persons to be brought if the previous condition fails.
segments.s269-g206: بیاریم و سازیم are two coordinated verbs in the jussive/subjunctive sense “let us bring / we should bring” and “we should make/prepare.” جای نبرد is the object “a place for battle” (جای = place; نبرد = battle). The coordination with و links the actions: bring (them) and prepare the battlefield.
segments.s269-g207: از این گفته‌گر marks source/agent: “from this storyteller/speaker.” بگسلی is a noun (from گسل/گسست) meaning “a rupture / a breaking” and functions as the event that affects دل. باز here = “again,” and دل is the affected theme “the heart.” The clause attributes to the teller’s words a new breaking/opening of the heart.
segments.s269-g208: من is the topical pronoun “I.” از گفته خود is an ablative/agentive phrase “from/by my own saying/words.” نیم is “half / partial” modifying دلگسل, an adjective meaning “heart‑rending / heart‑split.” The clause states the speaker’s subjective condition caused by his own words: ‘I am half-broken‑hearted by what I myself have said.’
segments.s269-g113: مرا (= me + object marker را) is the direct object (me). فرمود is a third-person past verb here with a causative/ordering sense ('ordered/caused'); the infinitive آویختن ('to hang') is used as the verbal complement/command (common in classical Persian: verb + infinitive as object). The conjunction و links the two coordinated actions ('ordered hanging' and what follows).
segments.s269-g114: به کین S+PP ('به' + noun) marks motive/aim here: 'out of/for hatred of Siavash'; خون ریختن is a light-verb construction with the infinitive ریختن ('to spill') taking the noun خون as its object ('to shed/spill blood'), forming a single action phrase that completes the clause.
segments.s269-g115: چو introduces a conditional clause here ('if'). فرمان خسرو is a genitive/possessive phrase 'Khosrow's order'. نیارم is an archaic/contracted negative first-person present ('نیاورم' → 'I do not bring' / 'I fail to bring'); به جای is the locative phrase 'to the place/right place' showing where the order should be brought.
segments.s269-g116: روان (the soul/spirit) is the subject; شرم دارد is a present/predicative construction 'has shame' → 'will be ashamed' (dardar expresses possession/state). به دیگر سرای is a prepositional phrase 'in/to another abode'—in context implying another world/afterlife. Word order is subject + predicate + locative complement.
segments.s269-g117: ور is conditional 'if' and اومید داری is present tense 'you have hope'. که introduces the subordinate clause. خسرو followed by the adverbial phrase به مهر ('by/through affection') indicates manner ('that Khosrow, through affection...'). The clause sets up a potential action to follow.
segments.s269-g118: گشاید is a third-person potential/optative/present verb form 'may open / will open' (here: 'be won over / open (his favour)'). بر این گفتها is the prepositional object 'to/upon these words' and بر تو چهر is a poetic inversion meaning 'turn/show his face toward you' (بر governs both phrases; چهر = face/visage).
segments.s269-g119: This noun phrase groups two items: گروگان ('the hostage') و آن خواسته ('and that/demand/request'), followed by هرچ هست ('whatever there is'); هست is the copula/existential 'is/are' asserting their state. The phrase functions as the topic that the simile in the next segment will describe.
segments.s269-g120: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. لهاک and رویین are descriptive nouns/adjectives ('eagerness/greed' and 'iron-faced/steel-hearted') and خسروپرست is a compound noun 'Khosrow-worshipper' (a sycophant/devotee of Khosrow). Together they liken the previously mentioned things to eager, iron-hearted Khosrow-devotees.
segments.s269-g105: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'. یاد آورم is a verbal phrase 'I bring to mind / I remember' (یاد + آورم), in present/imperfect (first person). چون کنم آشتی is an embedded question/retorical clause: چون 'how', کنم is the present/subjunctive first-person 'I do / I should do', and آشتی is the object 'reconciliation/peace' — so literally 'when I recall, how shall I make peace?'. Word order is typical poetic Persian (verb earlier) with the interrogative چون directly linked to کنَم.
segments.s269-g106: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('since' / 'for'). نیکی is the subject 'goodness/kindness', سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely', بدی is the object 'evil', and کاشتی is a past tense verb 'planted' (poetic/archaic past form). Together: 'for goodness has throughout planted evil.'
segments.s269-g107: به پنجم ('as to the fifth') is a topicalizing phrase meaning 'regarding the fifth point'. که گفتی is a reported clause 'that you said' (گفتی is 2nd-person past 'you said' — note the provided noun senses do not include this verbal sense). The following که + پیمان کنم is the subordinate content: که here introduces quoted content 'that', پیمان 'pact/pledge', and کنم is first-person subjunctive/hortative 'I should make' or 'I am to make'.
segments.s269-g108: ز توران specifies source/place 'from/of Turan' (poetic use of ز = from/about). سران را is the definite object 'the chiefs/leaders' marked by را, گروگان is the object complement 'hostage', and کنم is again the first-person verb 'I should/may take' (subjunctive). Word order places the place-source phrase before the object and verb in a poetic, emphatic arrangement.
segments.s269-g109: به نزدیک خسرو is a prepositional phrase 'to/near Khosrow' (به + نزدیک = 'to the vicinity of'). فرستیم is first-person plural (hortative/optative) 'let us send / we send', and گنج is the direct object 'treasure'. In poetry the verb may precede or follow the object; here the sense is a hortatory instruction to send treasure to the king's proximity.
segments.s269-g110: ببندیم is first-person plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us bind/let us fasten'. بر خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'upon ourselves' (بر = upon, خویشتن = ourselves), and راه رنج is a noun phrase 'the road/path of suffering' (راه head noun modified by رنج). Together: 'let us bind upon ourselves the path of suffering' — i.e., accept hardship.
segments.s269-g111: بدان‌ای is an imperative 'know this' with the vocative ای attached ('O ...'). نگهبان توران سپاه is a vocative phrase 'guardian/guard of Turan's army' (noun + possessor phrase); the order of nouns places the possessor after the noun in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s269-g112: که introduces the content clause 'that'. فرمان 'command/order' is the topic, جز اینست = 'is nothing other than this' (جز = except/other than; اینست = 'this is'), and ما را ز شاه supplies the beneficiaries/source: ما را = 'to us' (indirect object, marked by را) and ز شاه = 'from the king'. The overall meaning: 'that the command from the king to us is none other than this.'
segments.s269-g81: ندانی is an archaic/poetic second-person present with negative prefix ن- meaning "you do not know". کز introduces the subordinate/causal clause here (literally “from/through this...”); این = “this.” خیره modifies the action as a manner adverb meaning "dazed/astonished/reckless" (poetic use), and خون ریختن is a verbal noun phrase "the spilling/shedding of blood" (ریختن = to spill). The whole segment means “do you not know that by this reckless spilling of blood...”.
segments.s269-g82: گرفتار کردی is a transitive past form: گرفتـار (made/placed into a state of being seized/afflicted) + کردی (you did) = “you made (someone) seized/ensnared.” به marks the result or direction (“to/toward”), and فرجام تن = "the end/outcome of the body" (i.e. death/the body's end). The object (the one made گرفتار) is left implicit by poetic economy.
segments.s269-g83: من اکنون are the explicit subject and time adverb: “I now.” بدین is the classical combination of به + این meaning “by/with these,” and خوب‌گفتار (literally “good-speaking”) functions as a noun/adjective phrase meaning “fair/kind/eloquent words.” تو is the genitive/possessive "of you". The phrase as a whole = “I now, by these fair words of yours...”
segments.s269-g84: اگر introduces a conditional "if." باز گردم is a present-subjunctive/optative meaning “I turn back / I withdraw” (گردم = “I turn/go/return”); باز here means “back/away/again.” ز پیکار تو = “from your battle/struggle,” where ز (poetic variant of از) marks source/separation. The clause expresses the conditional "if I retreat from fighting you."
segments.s269-g85: به هنگام پرسش is a time phrase meaning “at the time of questioning” (به هنگام = at the time of). ز من uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) meaning “from/of me” and is the object phrase placed before the subject; کردگار (the Creator/God) is the subject that will perform the asking. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s269-g86: بپرسد is a third-person present/future form (بـ- prefix + پرسد) used here as “will ask”/“will question.” از marks the object/topic of asking (“about/concerning”), این گردش روزگار = “this turning/circuit of the times/fate” — گردش = turning/circuit and روزگار used poetically as “fate/times.” The full phrase means “(He) will ask about this turning of fate.”
segments.s269-g87: که here is the subordinating/relative particle 'that' introducing a list of items. The coordinated nouns سالاری و گنج و مردانگی are joined by و ('and') and serve as items being listed (they function as objects of the verb in the next segment). مردانگی is used in the sense of bravery/valor rather than literal masculinity.
segments.s269-g88: ترا is the 2nd-person object form made of the enclitic -ت plus the object marker را ('to/you' as the recipient). دادم is first-person singular past 'I gave'. The following و زور و فرزانگی continue the coordination ('and strength and wisdom') and are understood as additional things given by the same verb; زور = strength/might and فرزانگی = wisdom/sagacity.
segments.s269-g65: چه مایه is an exclamatory phrase («what a quantity/what many»), here introducing astonishment. بزرگان (the plural noun) is the head noun 'great ones / nobles'. که begins a relative clause describing those great men; از marks origin ('from'), and تخت و گاه are two coordinated nouns ('throne and seat/place') listing loci of rank or power. Word order is typical Persian: exclamation + head noun + relative clause introduced by که.
segments.s269-g66: This continues the relative clause: از ایران شدند uses شدند (past plural of شدن) meaning 'they became / they were' or simply 'were (from)'. اندر این کین تباه is the locative/result phrase 'in/within this enmity (they were) ruined' — اندر = 'in', این = 'this', کین = 'enmity/vengeance', تباه = predicate 'ruined/overthrown'. The verb is past and agrees with the plural subject implied by بزرگان.
segments.s269-g67: و دیگر ('and another') introduces an additional point. که گفتی (literally 'that you said') reports the addressee's words; the following که begins the reported clause. با پیر سر places 'با' ('with/against') before پیر سر ('old man' + 'head') — a fixed poetic way to say 'against an elder' or 'an old man's head' (i.e., to kill an old man). Note گفتی is reported speech (you said) although the provided lemma senses are nouny here.
segments.s269-g68: به خون ریختن is an infinitival/verbal noun phrase 'to shed blood' (به + noun/verb). کس نبندد کمر is a negative generic statement: کس = 'no one/anyone', نبندد is the classical-present/subjunctive-form meaning 'would gird/tighten' (here 'would take up arms, gird one's loins'), and کمر = 'waist/girdle' used idiomatically for 'gird oneself' (= prepare to fight). Together: 'no one would gird his loins to spill blood.'
segments.s269-g69: بدان‌ای is an imperative/address formula: بدان ('know/understand this') followed by the vocative ای. جهاندیده is a compound adjective 'worldly-wise / experienced', and پرفریب qualifies it 'deceptive / cunning' — together a direct address to the addressee (ironically praising/accusing him).
segments.s269-g70: This clause predicates the addressee's experience: به هر کار = 'in every affair', دیده is used as the past participle 'having seen' (i.e., 'you have seen'), and فراز و نشیب is a conventional pair meaning 'ups and downs / rises and falls'. Word order places the locus (به هر کار) before the experiential predicate (دیده فراز و نشیب).
segments.s269-g71: که introduces a clause explaining or justifying the speaker's circumstance: یزدان = 'God' (subject). مرا را is the object 'me' (here split in the tokenization as م + را); زندگانی دراز = 'a long life' is the object phrase. The overall sense: 'that God gave me a long life.' Note the tokenization anomalies (م + را) — grammatically this is the object marker on 'me'.
segments.s269-g72: بدان داد here functions as 'gave (it) to that / thereby gave' — the b- prefix (ب) and دان are part of an archaic/poetic construction (بدان = 'to that / thereby'), and داد is the past tense 'gave'. با introduces an instrumental phrase 'with/by', بخت = 'fortune/fate', and گردن‌فراز 'proud' qualifies بخت. Together: 'thereby God gave me long life with a proud/favourable fate.' Some tokenization items (ب, دان) are fragments of the particle بدَان and are treated as part of that adverbial idea.
segments.s269-g217: اگر introduces a conditional clause (‘if’). خواهی is second-person present 'you want' (classical/poetic present), so the clause reads 'if you want'. از من here means 'on my account / because of me' (object/beneficiary). زمان درنگ is a noun phrase 'a delay/a while' (literally 'time of delay'), acting as the thing desired in the conditional.
segments.s269-g218: وگر is the alternative conditional 'but/if on the other hand'. جنگ جویی is a verb phrase 'you seek war' (جویی = seek/pursue, 2nd-person form). بیارای is a verb form meaning 'array/prepare' (imperative/optative sense here: 'make ready'); the dictionary senses supplied do not match this verbal use. The final جنگ repeats the object 'war/ battle'.
segments.s269-g219: بدان is the fused classical form (به + آن / 'to that' or 'to him') used as the indirect object/recipient of گفتن; گفتم is the first-person past 'I said'. این is the direct object 'this'. تا introduces a temporal clause 'until', and به‌روز نبرد means 'the day of battle' ('به‌روز' = 'on the day', نبرد = 'battle'). The clause as a whole means 'I said this so that ... until the day of battle.'
segments.s269-g220: به ما بر بهانه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'against us on the pretext (of ...)' — بر here gives the sense 'against' and به ما marks the target 'us'. نبایدت کرد is a negative modal construction: نباید (must not / should not) + کرد (do/make), addressed to the addressee, meaning 'you must not make (such a pretext) against us.'
segments.s269-g221: که here introduces an explanatory/causal clause ('for' / 'because'); ناگاه is an adverb 'suddenly'. با ما به جنگ آمدی is subject + verb: با ما 'against/with us', به جنگ آمدی 'you came to fight / you came in battle' — آمدی is second-person past 'you came'.
segments.s269-g222: کمین کردی is a past-tense clause 'you made/laid an ambush' (کردی = you did). و links the actions, and بی‌درنگ آمدی is 'you came immediately' (بی‌درنگ = without delay, آمدی = you came). The sequence describes two rapid actions by the same actor.
segments.s269-g223: من این کین = 'I / my this grudge' (literally 'I, this grudge'); the phrase frames the topic. اگر تا به صد سالیان is a conditional temporal phrase 'if (even) until a hundred years' (تا = up to/until, صد سالیان = a hundred years). The whole segment sets up a hypothetical duration.
segments.s269-g224: بخواهم is subjunctive/present-tense first person 'if I will/wish' (here 'if I were to will/keep'), followed by همانست 'it is the same' (همان = 'the same', ست = copula 'is'). و اکنون همان repeats the state 'and now (it is) the same', emphasising that the grudge is unchanged even over the hypothetical long duration.
segments.s269-g193: بیازارد is a jussive/optative third‑person form of آزاریدن with the prefixed bi-/بـ (poetic variant), meaning 'may he harm/be displeased'; it stands at the start as the main clause verb. از من is a prepositional phrase 'at/with me' (از used in the sense 'concerning/with regard to'), and جهاندار شاه is an appositive noun phrase 'the world‑ruler, the king' (two epithets stacked). Word order (Verb — prepositional phrase — title) is typical of older poetic Persian.
segments.s269-g194: گر is the conditional 'if'. از یکدگر is a prepositional phrase 'from/away from one another'. بگسلانم is first person singular present/jussive with the verbal prefixed بـ ('I should / I were to tear/sever'), and it carries the conditional triggered by گر. سپاه is the direct object 'army/host' placed after the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s269-g195: آنک is a demonstrative/relative 'that' or 'which' introducing what follows. گفتی here functions metalinguistically ('you said / that you said'), but the provided sense list does not include the verbal 'you said', so the exact lexical sense is not listed; مبارز گزین is an imperative or exhortation 'choose a fighter' (گزین = 'choose' used as a command/infinitive), with مبارز 'fighter' as its object. The clause is compressed poetically: 'I/one said: choose that fighter (you spoke of)'.
segments.s269-g196: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' linking to مبارز. با من is the prepositional phrase 'with me'. بگردد is a present/subjunctive/relative verbal form (گردیدن) here with meaning 'go/ride/accompany' or 'be with' in context — it's a third‑person form agreeing with مبارز. بر این دشت کین is a locative phrase 'on/upon this plain of vengeance' (بر = 'on', این دشت = 'this plain', کین = 'vengeance' modifying the plain).
segments.s269-g197: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one' introducing a particular group. لشکری is 'an army / troops' (the noun in an adjectival or appositive position). پرگنه is an adjective 'full of sin/sinful' modifying لشکری. پیش من is the locative/phrasing 'before me' (پیش = 'before / in the presence of' + من = 'me'). The order (indefinite — noun — adjective — locative) reflects poetic compression.
segments.s269-g198: پرآزار is an adjective/noun meaning 'full of harm / very troubling' describing the effect. از ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'from / because of them' (از = 'from'), marking the source of the trouble. دل انجمن is a genitive construction 'the heart of the assembly' (دل = 'heart'; انجمن = 'assembly'), meaning the assembly was deeply troubled. Word order places the source (از ایشان) before the affected noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s269-g199: نباشد is a negative form of بودن but the supplied senses do not list the third‑person present 'is not', so the exact sense index is unavailable; here it functions as 'is not / let there not be'. ز من is a poetic genitive/agentive 'on my part / from me'. شاه همداستان is the noun phrase 'the king (being) like‑minded / in agreement' (همداستان = 'like‑minded'). Together the clause means 'the king is not of the same mind as I (am)'.
segments.s269-g200: کز is a fused conjunction 'that/which' introducing the subordinate clause. ایشان is 'them/they' as the object/source. بگردم is first person singular present/subjunctive of گردیدن here meaning 'I should turn/turn away' (used with the conditional/contrast introduced by نباشد). ب is the prepositional prefix 'to/into' (attached separately in the annotation), دین in the printed segmentation appears here as part of the demonstrative/particle in بدین but the provided senses for دین are unrelated, so its sense cannot be matched (-1). داستان is 'this matter / tale / affair' (here 'matter/affair'). The clause as a whole: 'that I should turn away from them in this matter.'
segments.s269-g281: بباید is an impersonal/modal verb meaning 'must/ought' (here marking necessity for the speaker); کنون is the adverb 'now' modifying that necessity. بست is the past-stem of بستن used as the verb-complement after the modal (classical pattern: بباید + stem → 'must bind/gird'). ما را is the first-person plural possessor/object with the object-marking را following ما (literally 'bind the waist of us'), and کمر is the noun 'waist/loins' (the thing to be bound) functioning as the direct object.
segments.s269-g282: نمانم is the negative first-person singular present/subjunctive form (ن + مانم) meaning 'I will not / I must not remain' or simply 'I shall not remain.' به here is a locative/relational preposition ('in/on, with respect to') linking the verb to the following place phrase. ایرانیان is the people/land-holder 'Iranians' and بوم و بر is a fixed phrase 'land and soil / homeland and domain' (بوم = homeland/soil, بر used here as 'land/territory'); together they indicate the place where the speaker will not remain.
segments.s269-g283: به here functions instrumentally/keying the means ('by/with'), followed by نیروی یزدان — a genitive construction meaning 'the power/force of God' (نیروی + یزدان). و links that phrase to شمشیر تیز, where the adjective تیز follows the noun (typical Persian order in poetry) to yield 'the sharp/keen sword.' The whole phrase names the instruments/means by which the action will be done.
segments.s269-g284: برآرم is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive or simple-present-as-future of برآوردن ('to raise, lift up'), here meaning 'I will raise' or 'I raise.' از introduces the source 'from' and آن انجمن is 'that assembly/assembly in question' (آن = that, انجمن = assembly). رستخیز is the object meaning 'resurrection / uprising' (here 'uprising'); word order places 'از آن انجمن' before the object for poetic emphasis, but syntactically the verb takes رستخیز as the thing raised, originating از آن انجمن.
segments.s269-g49: شنیدی is a 2nd-person singular perfect/past («Did you hear/Have you heard»). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). بر here is the preposition 'upon/against' used with a person to mark the one affected (بر ایرج = 'upon Irāj'). نیک‌بخت is an adjective (or epithet) following the name, literally 'fortunate' or 'well-fated'.
segments.s269-g50: چه آغاز سؤال/تعجبی 'what' asking about an event. آمد here is the verb 'came' / 'befell' (used idiomatically for 'happened'). ز (poetic form زِ) introduces source/agent ('from' or 'at the hands of') and تور is the proper name Tūr. از پی is a compound meaning 'in pursuit of' or 'seeking' (از = from/of, پی = following/after), followed by تاج و تخت 'crown and throne' as the object of that pursuit.
segments.s269-g51: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. از marks origin 'from' (here introducing the two names). سلم in this line is the name Salm (not the common noun senses listed), and اندر آمد is a verb phrase 'came into / entered' (اندر = 'into/within', آمد = 'came/arrived'). زمین is 'the land' or 'realm' and is the place entered.
segments.s269-g52: سراسر 'throughout' modifies the verb. بگسترد is an older/poetic 3rd-person verb form (بـ- prefix + گسترد) meaning 'spread' or 'was spread'; here it is equivalent to 'spread across'. بیداد and کین are abstract nouns 'injustice/oppression' and 'vengeance/hatred', the things that spread.
segments.s269-g53: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause about فریدون ('Fereydun, who ...'). از درد دل is a genitive-like construction 'from the pain of the heart' (از = 'from', درد = 'pain' used as emotional sorrow, دل = 'heart'). روز و شب 'day and night' is an adverbial phrase describing continuous suffering.
segments.s269-g54: گشادی here is a poetic/archaic verb form meaning 'he opened' (related to گشودن); the phrase دو لب گشادی literally 'he opened his two lips' = 'he spoke/uttered'. به نفرین ایشان means 'in/with a curse against them' (به marking the instrument or circumstance). لب 'lip' used literally of speaking.
segments.s269-g55: به + proper name (به افراسیاب) marks the experiencer/recipient 'to Afrasiab'; آمد is 'came' / 'befell'. آن مهر بد is a compact phrase where مهر can mean 'favour' or 'fortune' and بد qualifies it as 'bad' or 'ill' — 'that ill-favour/evil fortune'.
segments.s269-g56: از introduces the source/agent 'from'. آن نامداران refers to 'those renowned/famous men' (نامداران used as a plural noun), and اندک خرد is an adjectival phrase 'of little wisdom' (اندک = few/little, خرد = wisdom/intelligence), qualifying those men as illustrious but small-minded.
segments.s269-g233: سپهبد (spahbed) is the agent noun placed first for emphasis; بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he ordered/said'. تا introduces a subordinate (purpose/command) clause — here 'that' — and موبدان is the noun (the priests) that begins that subordinate clause. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but grammatically: Agent + verb + conjunction + subordinate subject.
segments.s269-g234: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; لشکر is the host/army; همه 'all' modifies the following adjective; نامور is an adjective 'renowned' that modifies the following noun; بخردان here is used as a plural substantive (the 'wise ones'), i.e. 'wise/prudent ones', not the verbal sense. The phrase gives the source ('from the host') and the group to be fetched.
segments.s269-g235: به زودی is an adverbial phrase 'soon/quickly' (به here functions to form the adverbial); سوی + noun means 'toward (the) ...'. آمدند is the past simple third‑person plural 'they came'. The temporal adverb precedes the direction and verb, typical of Persian.
segments.s269-g236: This repeats the subject/arrival with a list of descriptors: خردمند 'the wise' and روشن‌روان 'the bright‑souled/clear‑minded' joined by و 'and', followed by آمدند 'they came/arrived'. The adjectival nouns stand in apposition to the implied visitors; verb agreement is plural.
segments.s269-g237: پس is a connective 'then/thereupon'. آن پاسخ‌نامه 'that reply‑letter' is the direct object/topic of the next clause. پیش here means 'in the presence of/before', and گوان is a proper/name or reference placed after پیش to mark before whom the letter is to be read.
segments.s269-g238: بفرمود again is the honorific past 'he commanded/ordered'. خواندن is the infinitive 'to read' (object of the command). همی is an archaic/aspectual particle used here to mark the action as performed or imminent (classical Persian usage). پهلوان is the person specified to perform the reading (the hero); the word order is inverted for poetic effect but yields 'he ordered that the hero read it.'
segments.s269-g239: بزرگان 'the great ones/elders' is the subject; که introduces a relative/temporal clause 'when/that'; آن نامه دلپذیر 'that pleasing letter' is the object of hearing inside the clause. Adjectival sequence places دلپذیر after نامه as an attribute.
segments.s269-g240: شنیدند is the past third‑person plural 'they heard/listened'. گفتار 'speech/words' is the direct object and is linked to the following name as its source: فرخ دبیر (Farrokh the scribe). Here فرخ functions as a proper name (lexical sense 'fortunate' available but used as a name), and دبیر specifies his office.
segments.s269-g89: This is a prepositional/agent phrase: به (be) here marks purpose/aim 'for/to' (i.e., 'to avenge'); کین (kīn) means 'vengeance' and takes a following noun سیاوش (Siyâvash) in genitive relationship ('Siavash's vengeance' / 'to avenge Siavash'). کمر (kamar) 'waist' is the noun that combines with the omitted verb بستن ('to gird') to form the idiom 'gird one's loins'; بر میان literally 'upon the midst' (بر = 'against/over', میان = 'middle') gives a martial sense 'to set oneself in the midst / prepare for action'.
segments.s269-g90: نبستی (nabasti) is the negative past second‑person singular of بستن 'to bind/gird' — here 'you did not gird (your loins)'. چرا (cherâ) is the interrogative 'why'. پیش ایرانیان places the action 'before/in the presence of the Iranians' (پیش = 'in front of / in the presence of'). The verb comes as a single-word predicate in classic poetic word order after the topic phrase.
segments.s269-g91: به هفتاد خون گرامی پسر is a topical noun phrase: به here functions like 'concerning/about' or 'regarding'; هفتاد خون 'seventy bloods' means seventy clans/lineages (خون = 'blood' = kinship line); گرامی پسر is a compound modifier 'honored/esteemed sons' (adjective + noun). Together they form the theme 'regarding the honored sons of the seventy clans.'
segments.s269-g92: بپرسد (beporsad) is a present/subjunctive/optative third‑person form often rendered 'let him ask' or 'may he ask' in classical Persian (though the gloss list gives 'asked'); ز من = 'from me' (ز = 'from', من = object 'me'); داور دادگر is a nominal subject in apposition 'the judge, the just one' (داور = 'judge', دادگر = 'just' or 'upright'). Word order is verb + prepositional object + subject for poetic effect.
segments.s269-g93: ز پاسخ (z-e pâsokh) means 'as for an answer' or 'in reply'; به پیش (be pīsh) is 'in the presence of / before'; جهان‌آفرین is a compounded epithet 'the Creator' or 'world‑maker'. The whole prepositional phrase sets up the scene 'what answer before the Creator.'
segments.s269-g94: This contains two closely linked questions. چه گویم begins a rhetorical question 'what shall I say' — گویم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of گفتن 'to say' (here the gloss list lacks that verbal sense, so the exact sense isn't listed). چرا بازگشتم ز کین is 'why did I return/come back because of vengeance' — بازگشتم is past tense 'I returned', ز = 'because of / from', کین = 'vengeance'.
segments.s269-g95: ز کار سیاوش is a genitive/relational phrase 'from the deeds/acts of Siavash' (ز = 'from/regarding', کار = 'deed/affair', سیاوش = proper name). چهارم سخن 'the fourth speech/tale' is a numeral + noun phrase identifying which of the tales is meant; Persian permits this order in poetry.
segments.s269-g96: که (ke) introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. افگندی (afgandī) is a second‑person past form 'you cast/threw/uttered' used here as 'you related/uttered' in reference to a tale. ای پیر سالار is a vocative 'O old lord/commander' (ای = 'O!', پیر = 'old/elder', سالار = 'chief/commander'). بن at the end is likely a proper name or epithet in vocative; none of the provided dictionary senses fit that personal use, so it is treated as a vocative/name here.
segments.s269-g273: This pre-verbal phrase sets up the agent and object of the upcoming verbal action. چرا (why) begins a rhetorical question; من is the 1st-person subject; به + کین indicates purpose/aim 'for vengeance'; برادر is the target ('brother'); کمر is the noun that combines with بستن (to gird) in the idiom کمر بستن ('to gird one's loins/make ready'). Word order in Persian places this whole noun phrase before the verb.
segments.s269-g274: نبندم is 1st-person singular (classical/persian-subjunctive/optative/epistolary) form of بستن used here in a rhetorical negative question sense ('should I not gird'). نخارم is negative + 1st-person of the archaic verb خاریدن (here figurative: 'to be pricked/ to flinch/ be humiliated'), meaning 'I will not shrink / I will not be humiliated'. از این کینه سر is a small prepositional phrase ('from/of this hatred's source/head') where از marks origin or cause and سر refers to the head/source of the grudge. The two verbs together express: 'Should I not gird myself ...? I will not be deterred by the source of this hatred.'
segments.s269-g275: هم introduces an additive/emphatic idea ('also' / 'even'). از governs خون ('from/for the blood of ...'), and نهصد سر نامدار is a numerical noun phrase 'nine hundred renowned heads' (name + noun; adjective نامدار follows the noun it modifies in this line). The phrase supplies another motive or grievance: 'also for the blood of nine hundred famed heads.'
segments.s269-g276: که is a relative/linking particle 'which/that' referring back to the heads. از تن جدا شد is a passive/impersonal description 'were separated from the body' (جدا + شد: became separated). گه کارزار is temporal/adverbial 'at times of battle' (گه = at times, کارزار = battle). The whole relative clause explains how those heads were lost: 'which were at times in battle severed from their bodies.'
segments.s269-g277: که again introduces a clause ('that/for that'), اندر means 'in/within', and بر و بوم is a binomial expression for 'land; soil; homeland' (بر و بوم ترکان = the Turks' land). دگر here modifies the following noun ('other/another'). Word order places the locative phrase before the predicate.
segments.s269-g278: سواری is the subject 'a rider/horseman'. چو + هومان is a simile 'like Homan'. نبندد is 3rd-person singular present/optative of بستن (here negative: 'will not/does not gird'), and کمر is the object in the idiom کمر بستن ('gird one's loins'). Together: 'another rider like Homan will not gird his loins (there).' The negative expresses absence of such a figure among the Turks.
segments.s269-g279: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction 'when' or 'as'. نستیهن is a proper name (the person referred to). آن سرو is a poetic epithet 'that cypress' (a conventional simile for a tall, graceful man), سایه + فگن is a compound descriptive: سایه 'shade' and فگن (an archaic verbal/adjectival form) here modifies سایه to mean something like 'shade-casting' or 'shade-making' (i.e. a figure who casts/throws a shade). The whole noun phrase is a hashed epithet describing Nestihehn.
segments.s269-g280: که introduces the result clause 'so that/and then'. شد ناپدید is a two-word predicate 'became/got vanished' (شد + adjective ناپدید = 'disappeared'). از همه انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from every assembly/gathering' (از marking separation or removal, همه 'all/every', انجمن 'assembly'). The clause says he disappeared from all gatherings.
segments.s269-g17: ولیکن is a conjunction 'but/yet' introducing a contrast. نه precedes گاه to negate it: 'not a time'. فریبست is a nominal-predicate (فریب + ـست) meaning 'is deception' or 'is a matter of deceit' and stands after the negated temporal noun. و links بند (fetter, bond) as a second noun in the list of things that this is not the time for.
segments.s269-g18: که here gives the explanatory/causal sense 'for' / 'since'. هنگام is the temporal noun 'at the time (of)' and governs the nominal predicate that follows. گرزست is nominal + copula (گرز + ـست) meaning 'is a mace' or 'is the mace/time of the mace' (poetic nominal sentence). و links تیغ and کمند, simple nouns in a list: 'mace and sword and lasso/snare'.
segments.s269-g19: مرا با تو is a topical prepositional phrase 'as for me with you / between you and me'. The segment ends with جز ... نیست, an existential negation meaning 'there is nothing except ...'. جز introduces the excluded items (کین و پیکار). Note that را here is the object/definite marker clitic attached to مرا in the original; word order is topical and the main copula/negation appears as نیست at the end.
segments.s269-g20: گه here means 'occasion; time' (not 'sometimes'). پاسخ و روز گفتار نیست is another negation: 'there is no answer and no time/day for speech' — روز گفتار is a set phrase meaning 'time for speaking' or 'a day for talk'. The final نیست negates both nouns.
segments.s269-g21: نگر is the imperative 'look!/consider!'. تا چه سان is a fixed interrogative phrase meaning 'how / to what degree' introducing the subordinate clause. گردد is a present/future subjunctive/impersonal verb 'becomes/will become' (گرد + ـد), and اکنون سپهر places the time and subject after it: 'now the heavens'. The clause order is poetic (imperative + subordinate question + verb + subject/time).
segments.s269-g22: نه جای negates 'place' — 'this is not the place/occasion for ...'. فریبست is a nominal-predicate (فریب + ـست) 'is deception' or 'a place of deceit' and is coordinated with the following nouns via و: پیوند 'bond/tie' and مهر 'love/affection'. The whole phrase lists what is inappropriate in the present circumstances.
segments.s269-g23: کرا is the archaic interrogative/relative 'whom/what'. داد خواهد is a poetic, inverted future construction: داد (root 'give') plus خواهد ('will') — together 'will give' though the order is inverted for meter. جهاندار is the agent 'the world-ruler / sovereign' and زور is the noun 'strength/might' tied to the action; overall: 'whom the sovereign will give (strength)'.
segments.s269-g24: کرا again 'whom'. بردهد is a poetic verb form (here functioning in the clause that follows the first), and the clause assigns the opposing action to fate: بخت پیروز هور is a nominal phrase 'victorious fortune / the victorious luck of splendour' acting as subject/agent — 'victorious Fortune's splendour will (take/carry) [him] off.' Word order and some verbal meanings are archaic/poetic; the clause contrasts what rulers give with what fortune takes.
segments.s269-g265: Two nouns joined by و (and) form a coordinated direct object/phrase (شکیبایی و خامشی). The verb برگزید is the simple past of برگزیدن (to choose); word order is [objects] then verb, a common poetic inversion where the chosen qualities are stated before the past-tense verb 'chose.'
segments.s269-g266: بکرد is an archaic/poetic past form (ب‑ + کرد) meaning 'he did / he made.' آن سخن is the theme ('that speech'), then the prepositional phrase بر سپه ('before/on the host') and the predicate adjective ناپدید ('vanished') follows; the clause means the speech was rendered unseen/vanished in front of the army.
segments.s269-g267: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / from that time on.' چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین + گفت), where گفت is the past-tense verb 'he said' (note: the provided lemma senses are for noun uses of گفت, so the verbal sense is the relevant one). پیش سپاه means 'in the presence of the army / before the host' and functions as an adverbial phrase.
segments.s269-g268: که is the conjunction introducing reported speech ('that'). گودرز را uses را to topicalize/mark Goudarz; دل is the grammatical 'heart' subject of the idiom دل نیامد (literally 'the heart did not come') meaning 'the heart did not incline' or 'he was not willing.' نیامد is past negative 'did not come/occur.' به راه 'to the road/way' expresses the direction or cause (i.e. inclined toward the course).
segments.s269-g269: از آن خون is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of that blood.' هفتاد پور گزین reads as 'seventy chosen sons' (پور = son/descendant; گزین used adjectivally 'chosen/selected'); note the classical order: [of X] [number + noun + modifier].
segments.s269-g270: نیارامدش is a negative past construction meaning 'it did not calm/quiet him' with the enclitic ‑ش ('him/his') attached; word order places the verb before the time expression یک زمان ('one moment') and the phrase دل ز کین ('the heart from vengeance') states what did not find rest — i.e. his heart did not rest from/for vengeance even for a moment.
segments.s269-g271: گر is the conditional 'if.' ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning something like 'even now' or 'still so' and modifies the clause; او is the subject pronoun 'he.' بر گذشته سخن is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'concerning past words' (بر + گذشته + سخن), placing the topic that arouses the feeling.
segments.s269-g272: به نوی 'in a new way / anew'; همی is a classical continuous/iterative marker (here 'still / continues to'); کینه سازد is a present/subjunctive-like or habitual-form verb 'he makes/raises/harbors hatred' (سازد = 'creates/makes'); ز بن 'from the root' ('at the root' = fundamentally). Together the clause says he again and still cultivates hatred at its root.
segments.s269-g73: که is a relative/conjunctive particle ('that, who') introducing the clause. از شهر توران is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the city of Turan' (preposition + noun). به‌روز is an adverbial phrase 'in/by the day, by daylight' modifying نبرد; نبرد here functions as a noun/verb root meaning 'battle/engage in battle' in an older, compact poetic form (word order is prepositional/adverbial phrase then the action noun/verb).
segments.s269-g74: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/out of' giving cause. کینه is the object 'grudge/hatred'. برآرم is a first‑person present/future sense 'I raise / I will lift up' (classical prefix بر- + آرم). به introduces the target/direction. خورشید گرد is a compound phrase: خورشید 'sun' and گرد here as 'around/encircling' (i.e. 'around the sun' or 'to the sun's circle'); together they express where the raised grudge is turned.
segments.s269-g75: بترسم is a first‑person verb 'I fear' (classical/poetic present). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'indeed/still' that intensifies the verb. زانک is a causal conjunction 'because/for' introducing the reason clause that follows; یزدان من literally 'my God' or 'God (for me)' and here names the agent of the following action.
segments.s269-g76: ز is the preposition 'from'. تن 'body' is the source from which something is torn. بگسلاند is a third‑person (optative/subjunctive/hortative) form 'may (he) detach/loosen/separate' (classical verbal morphology). مگر here is a small particle whose exact classical nuance is concessive/assimilative (rendered in sense by context); جان من 'my life/soul' is the thing that may be separated. Overall the clause expresses the fear that God might separate the speaker's life from his body.
segments.s269-g77: من is the subject 'I'. این کینه را is the definite object 'this grudge' (این + noun + را as object marker). ناوریده is a classical adjective/participle meaning 'not brought/untested/not yet acted on' and به جای ('to/into place') expresses the idea of 'into its proper place' — together: 'I have not yet put this grudge in its place.'
segments.s269-g78: بر و بومتان is a compound reference to 'on/against your homeland' (بر 'on/upon' + بومتان 'your native soil' with the 2nd‑person possessive). ناسپرده is a negative participle meaning 'not entrusted/not delivered' and به پای 'to the foot/at the feet' is a locative phrase; the clause says he has not laid or entrusted the grudge upon/at your land's feet — i.e. he has not vented it toward your homeland.
segments.s269-g79: سه دیگر is a discourse marker 'another thing / one more (matter)'. که introduces the reported content 'that which' and گفتی is the verb 'you said' in the classical diction (here used nominally in the phrase 'that you said'); ز یزدان پاک means 'about/from the pure God' (ز = concerning/of). The whole segment sets up the quoted/mentioned point the speaker is responding to.
segments.s269-g80: نبینم is negative-locution 'I do not see' (classical present). به دلت is 'in your heart' (به + دل + 2nd‑person suffix ت). اندرون 'within' reinforces the interior domain. بیم و باک are two near-synonyms 'fear and dread/anxiety' joined by و. Grammatically, the clause states the speaker's observation that no fear is present in the addressee's inner heart.
segments.s269-g153: A vocative phrase addressing the addressee: تو‌ای is the poetic vocative 'O thou'; نامور is an adjective 'renowned'; پهلوان سپاه is a nominal phrase 'champion of the host' formed by juxtaposing the head noun (پهلوان) with its qualifying noun (سپاه) without an explicit ezāfe — normal in epic diction.
segments.s269-g154: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه 'gaze/look' + کن imperative 'do' = 'look, fix your gaze' or 'keep watch'. بدین گردش is a prepositional phrase 'by/at this turning/circuit' (بدین = به + این in older spelling), گردش 'turn/rotation'; هور و ماه is a poetic pair 'sun (splendour) and moon'. The phrase attaches as the object/place of the imperative.
segments.s269-g155: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that'. بند سپهری is a compound meaning 'a heavenly/celestial stanza/verse' (بند here = stanza/verse); فراز آمدست is a poetic perfect/present-stative 'آمدست' = 'has come up/risen', with فراز 'on high' placed before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s269-g156: سر بخت ترکان is a genitival noun chain: سر 'top/summit' + بخت 'fortune' + ترکان 'of the Turks' = 'the summit/top of the Turks' fortune'. به گاز آمدست is the verb phrase 'has come to/into gāz' (به = to/toward; آمدست = archaic perfect 'has come'), where گاز is a locus/term whose precise meaning is obscure here (left as a lexical item or place).
segments.s269-g157: نگر is an imperative 'look/consider'. تا introduces the clause that follows in the sense 'so that / in order to see' or colloquially 'see what'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'; کردار 'deed(s)'; بدگوهرت is an adjective-noun 'bad-gem' used as 'ill‑natured / of evil character' describing the person addressed. Together: 'Look, so that from your evil deeds...'.
segments.s269-g158: چه is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what/how' introducing the content of the observation; آرد is the classical present/epic form of آوردن in the third person singular 'brings / will bring' (used to state what will befall); جهان‌آفرین is the agent 'the world‑creator' (God/creator); بر سرت is the prepositional phrase 'upon your head' meaning 'to you' or 'over you'.
segments.s269-g159: زمانه functions as an impersonal/abstract subject here ('time' → 'fortune' or 'the world'). ز بد 'from bad / because of the bad' and دامن اندر کشید is a poetic sequence meaning 'drew its hem/embrace inward' or 'withdrew its protection' (اندر کشید = pulled in). کشید is a past verb 'drew/pulled' in a poetic register (the precise verbal sense in the provided lemma list does not match exactly).
segments.s269-g160: مکافات بد را is a topicalized object: 'the punishment/retribution for evil' marked by را. بد آید پدید is an idiomatic poetic sequence: آید 'comes' (present/future sense) + پدید 'forth/into view' = 'appears/comes to light'; the preceding بد qualifies the retribution as evil/bad or indicates 'for the wicked'. Overall: 'the retribution for evil appears.'
segments.s269-g129: سوی ... تا ... is a direction-range phrase: سوی + place gives ‘toward’ and تا introduces the limit ‘as far as’. Here سوی باختر = ‘toward the west’, and تا به مرز خزر = ‘as far as the Khazar frontier’. Word order is typical Persian prepositional: direction (سوی) + place (باختر) then the limiting تا phrase.
segments.s269-g130: همه گشت is ‘all became/turned’; گشت is the past/simple verbal idea ‘became/turned’. لهراسب را uses را as the definite/object/topic marker attached to the name (here marking Lohrasp as the affected party). سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning ‘head by head’ / ‘completely / one after another’ (used poetically to indicate totality or every head). The sequence reads: ‘all (were) turned Lohrasp’s (men) head by head’ — i.e. Lohrasp’s forces were entirely routed.
segments.s269-g131: سوی + نیمروز = ‘toward Nimruz / toward the south’; اندرون (‘inside/inward’) follows to mark movement into the interior. تا introduces the limit ‘until’ (temporal/durational) and links to بسند ‘to suffice’ (here meaning ‘until it sufficed’ or ‘until they had done/enough’). Word order keeps the directional phrase up front, then the limiting clause.
segments.s269-g132: جهان شد is the subject + past copula ‘the world became’. به کردار = ‘in the manner/appearance of’ (به + noun used idiomatically to mark likeness), followed by روی پرند ‘the face/aspect of a bird’ (روی = face/aspect, پرند = winged one/bird). Together: ‘the world became in the manner/appearance of a bird.’
segments.s269-g133: This noun phrase lists instruments/agents around Rostam. نیو = ‘ney’ (reed‑flute) and با تیغ تیز = ‘with a sharp sword’. The initial token تهم is not transparent here (it is rare/archaic and does not match its usual sense ‘slander’), so it likely functions as a poetic or dialectal element or an alternate reading; the core grammatical construction is رستم (subject) followed by instrument phrases.
segments.s269-g134: برآورد is the verb ‘brought forth/caused/raised’ (here ‘raised/awoke’). از ایشان = ‘from them’ (preposition + pronoun) marks the source, دم = ‘breath/moment/cry’ (here figuratively ‘cry/impetus’), and رستخیز = ‘uprising / (resurrection)’ (used here in a martial sense: a rising/clamor for battle). Word order is standard: verb after subject phrase, then source and object of what was raised.
segments.s269-g135: سر هندوان is a genitive construction where سر (‘head’) is used with هندوان (‘the Indians’) to mean the heads (i.e. beheaded heads or chiefs) of the Indians; Persian often uses singular nouns in such set phrases. با درفش سیاه is an accompanying instrument/attribute phrase ‘with a black banner/standard’ (با + noun + adjective).
segments.s269-g136: فرستاد رستم = ‘Rostam sent’ (simple past transitive verb + subject). به نزدیک شاه = ‘to the vicinity/near the king’ (به + نزدیک ‘near’ + شاه ‘king’). Object (the thing sent) appears before the verb, a normal poetic/neutral ordering in Persian.
segments.s269-g185: به فرجام is an adverbial phrase meaning “in the end / at last.” گفتی here is a poetic past-tense reportive (used like ‘he said’ in Ferdowsi) and ز مردانِ مرد is a genitive-from phrase: ز = “from/of,” مردان = “men,” and مرد is an epithet ‘man among men’ (a heroic superlative), so together ‘from the men — the man (i.e. the best of men).’
segments.s269-g186: تنی چند is an idiom meaning ‘a few (men)’ (تنی used as ‘some/a certain’). بگزین is an imperative ‘choose’ (root گزین), followed by ز بهر نبرد = ‘for the sake of battle’ (ز = ‘from/of’, بهر = ‘for’, نبرد = ‘battle’). Word order is typical Persian: object/quantity then verb then purpose phrase.
segments.s269-g187: This is a topicalised subject/agent line: من = “I.” از لشکر ترک = “from the Turk army/host” (از = from/of). هم = “also/too.” ز این نشان = “by/with this sign/token” (ز = from/by, این نشان = this token); the pieces set up the source and motivation of what follows.
segments.s269-g188: بیارم is a first-person verbal form meaning ‘I will bring / I bring’ (the poet uses the b- prefix to make the present/subjunctive/future sense). Sواران = ‘horsemen/riders’ is the direct object; مردم‌کشان is an epithet ‘slayers of men’ modifying the horsemen. The clause thus states the action promised by the subject.
segments.s269-g189: The first که begins a causal clause here (‘for/because’). از مهربانی = ‘out of kindness.’ The second که introduces the subordinate phrase ‘which (is) toward my army’ (relative/locution): بر لشکرم = ‘toward/upon my army’ with the possessive -م ‘my’ attached to لشکر.
segments.s269-g190: نخواهم is the first-person negative desiderative ‘I will not / I do not wish.’ The following که introduces the content of that wish (‘that ...’). بیداد کین = two nouns in coordination ‘injustice/oppression and vengeance’ and گسترم is the first-person present/subjunctive of گستراندن ‘to spread / to extend,’ so the clause says what he does not want to do.
segments.s269-g191: تو = ‘you.’ با مهربانی = ‘with kindness / kindly’ (ablative/manner). نهی here is a prohibitive/negative-imperative idea ‘do not (advance) / restrain’ (used as an exhortation). پای پیش literally ‘the forward foot’ = ‘to step forward / advance’; the phrase means ‘do not step forward’ or ‘hold your step.’
segments.s269-g192: که = ‘for/because.’ دانی is second-person present ‘you know.’ نهان دل و رای خویش = ‘(the) hidden heart and counsel of your own’ — نهان = ‘hidden,’ دل = ‘heart,’ و = ‘and,’ رای = ‘counsel/plan,’ and خوی + ش form خویش ‘one’s own’ (here the suffix ش is the third-person/possessive element meaning ‘your…’ in context ‘your own’). The clause gives the reason for restraining the step.
segments.s269-g25: ولیکن is an adversative particle 'but'. بدین here is the older fused preposition phrase به + این = 'to/at this', modifying گفته 'statement'. پاسخ شنو places the noun 'پاسخ' (reply) before the imperative verb 'شنو' ('listen!/hear') — a poetic/object-before-verb order meaning 'listen to the reply'.
segments.s269-g26: This is an imperative compound: خرد یاد کن = 'remember wisdom' where یاد کن is literally 'make remembrance' (imperative 'do/recall'). بخت را پیشرو is a second imperative/command complement meaning 'make fortune the forerunner' — را marks 'بخت' as the object and پیشرو is the complement ('forerunner/leader'), so together: 'remember wisdom, and set/let fortune go before you.'
segments.s269-g27: نخست is an adverb 'first'. آنک is an archaic relative/conjunction 'that / which', introducing the reported content. گفتی here is the report verb ('you said' / 'it was said'), and که begins the subordinate clause; از مهر نیز = 'from / because of love as well' (نیز = 'also'). The clause is an archaic narrative register and the word order places the topical particle/relative before the verb.
segments.s269-g28: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from'. یزدان = 'the gods / God' as a source. وز is a fused poetic item (effectively 'and from' = و + ز) linking another source; گردش رستخیز = 'the turning/circuit of resurrection' where گردش ('turn, motion') modifies رستخیز ('resurrection/uprising'). Together they list sources ('from the gods and from the movement/turning of resurrection').
segments.s269-g29: نخواهم is the present/past-fused negative of 'to want' meaning 'I do not/will not wish'. The clause نخواهم که آید uses the subjunctive/present آید after که to express the undesired event ('that it should come / that I should come'). مرا (here split as م + را) is the object 'me' and precedes the verb in poetic order; پیش جنگ = 'before battle'.
segments.s269-g30: دلم = 'my heart' (دل + possessive clitic -م). گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' ('became' here). از این کار = 'because of this deed/affair' and بیداد تنگ is a descriptive phrase: بیداد 'injustice' with تنگ 'narrow/tight' forming the idiom 'دل تنگ' (heart made tight/sorrowful/angry). The whole means 'my heart became constricted by this injustice.'
segments.s269-g31: دلت is 'your heart' (دل + 2nd-person suffix ت). با زبان آشنایی = 'acquaintance/familiarity with language/speech' (با = 'with'). نداشت is the past negative of داشتن 'did not have'. The clause thus says 'your heart did not have familiarity with words/speech' — i.e. it was unaccustomed to speaking or to such expressions.
segments.s269-g32: بدان گه is an adverbial phrase 'at that time/at that occasion' (بدان = به + آن in older form; گه = 'occasion/time'). که introduces the relative clause 'that/when this saying ...'. این گفته بر دل گماشت = 'this saying placed upon the heart' where بر دل is the prepositional object 'upon the heart' and گماشت is the past 'placed/laid/entrusted' (active verb often with a sense of impressing something upon someone).
segments.s269-g57: ز سر is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning "from the head" = "from the outset / at the start." The preposition ز governs سر (noun). با + proper name marks accompaniment/association ("with Manuchehr"); نو modifies کین ("a new / fresh hatred"), and نهاد is the simple past (3rd sg.) of نهادن "to place / put / establish," with an omitted subject (understood from context). Word order is preposition + noun, then 'با + name', then adjective + noun, then verb.
segments.s269-g58: همیدون is a proper name (subject). ابا here is used as a verb in an older register meaning "to refuse / be reluctant" (S V order). نوذر و کیقباد are the coordinated objects (Nowzar and Kay Qobad) linked by و "and."
segments.s269-g59: به کاووس sets Kavus as the indirect object/direction ("to/against Kavus"). کی here functions in the clause as an interrogative/indefinite/archaic particle in context but the whole phrase reads as a nominal clause with کرد (past "did"); خود is emphatic "himself" or "personally," and آنچ (those things / that which) is the object; the final کرد is the main past verb "did." The clause therefore says roughly "he himself did that to/against Kavus."
segments.s269-g60: برآورد is a past transitive verb meaning "brought / effected / took (away)" here; از introduces the source "from" and ایران آباد is the noun phrase "prosperous Iran." گرد is an archaic/poetic element completing the verbal idea (here giving a sense of motion/turning or result), so the whole clause means roughly "he brought ruin / removed the prosperous Iran." Word order is verb – preposition – source noun phrase – final archaic particle.
segments.s269-g61: از آن پس is a temporal phrase "after that / thereafter." به + کین + سیاووش expresses the target/direction of hatred ("toward the enmity against Siyavash"). باز is an adverb "again" modifying the action in the next segment. The clause sets the time and the renewed direction of hostility.
segments.s269-g62: فگند is an archaic past form (from فکندن) meaning "threw / cast" (3rd sg.). این‌چنین "thus / in this way" modifies the verb or the manner, and کینه نو دراز is a noun phrase "a long new grudge" where نو and دراز are postpositive adjectives modifying کینه. The literal image is "he cast such a long new hatred."
segments.s269-g63: نیامد is a negative past construction meaning "did not come / it did not occur." بدانگه is an adverbial phrase "at that time / thereupon." ترا is split orthographically into ت (second-person clitic "to you") + را (object/emphatic marker); داد here is the verb "gave" and یاد is the noun "reminder / recollection." Together the clause means "you were not given/reminded (lit. 'it did not come to you a remembrance')."
segments.s269-g64: که introduces a subordinate content clause explaining what was not recalled. او is the subject "he," بی‌گنه is an epithet "innocent" placed before the verbal phrase, جان شیرین is a noun phrase "dear / sweet life" (شیرین functioning as an epithet of جان), and بداد is a past verb meaning "gave" (here idiomatically "gave up his life" / "lost his life"). The clause therefore reads "that he, innocent, gave up his dear life."
segments.s269-g41: Subject + verb structure with a fronted definite object. تو (you) is the subject, کردی is the simple past 2nd-person 'you did'. همه جنگ را is the definite object marked by را ('the whole war'). دست پیش is a compound noun meaning 'the initiative' or 'the first move' and here functions as a complement describing what was 'done' to the war (i.e. you 'took the initiative'). Word order is Subject–Verb but Persian allows the object phrase to appear before such a complement.
segments.s269-g42: سپه را is a topicalized definite object ('the army'), marked with را. تو appears after it as the subject; برکندی is the past tense (2nd-person) of برکندن 'to uproot/dislodge' (a transitive past). از جای خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from its place/position' where خویش is formed from خوی + the pronominal suffix ش meaning 'its/his/her', giving 'its own place'.
segments.s269-g43: This is a conditional clause introduced by the archaic/poetic conditional particle ار ('if'). خرد is the topic 'wisdom'. پس آمد is 'came after' (پس = 'after' + آمد = 'came'). تو پیش آمدی is the main clause: تو subject and پیش آمدی 'you came before/you took precedence' (پیش آمدن = to come forward / take precedence). The sentence uses the prototypical Old/poetic Persian conditional ordering.
segments.s269-g44: به marks direction/result 'to/into' and governs فرجام آرام 'a calm outcome/end' (فرجام = end/outcome, آرام = calm). بیش modifies آمدی as a degree adverb meaning 'more' or 'to a greater degree' and 함께 with آمدی (you came/arrived) gives 'you arrived to a calmer/more peaceful end'—i.e. you ended up in a calmer fate. Word order places the prepositional goal (به فرجام آرام) before the verb.
segments.s269-g45: ولیکن is a formal/poetic adversative conjunction 'but/yet'. سرشت بد و خوی بد is a coordinated noun phrase with two nouns ('bad nature' and 'bad disposition'), each modified by the adjective بد 'bad'. The two qualities are paralleled by و 'and' to emphasize the subject.
segments.s269-g46: ترا here is the object form 'you' (written in the data as the 2nd-person suffix ت plus the object marker را); نگذراند is a present-perfective/past-form verb meaning 'did not let/ did not allow' (in the sense 'caused not to pass' — نگذاشت / نگذراند). به راه خرد is the prepositional phrase 'onto the way/path of wisdom' (راه = path, خرد = wisdom). The verb governs the object ترا and the prepositional complement.
segments.s269-g47: بدی خود is the nominal subject 'its/one's own evil' (خود = own/itself). بدان here functions as an older/poetic locative/demonstrative ('in/at that') (written in the data as ب + دان), followed by تخمه 'seed/stock'. در گوهرست is a nominal predicate 'is in the essence' (در = in, گوهرست = 'is a gem' / 'is in the essence' in poetic usage). Overall: 'Its evil is in that seed's essence.'
segments.s269-g48: به بد کردن is a verbal-nominal construction 'by making bad / by corrupting' (به + verbal noun کردن). آن تخمه 'that seed' is the patient; اندر is a poetic preposition 'within/in' and خورست is a stative/passive-like predicate 'is eaten / is consumed' (here figuratively 'is ruined/consumed'). The whole clause means 'by corrupting that seed, it is consumed/ruined.'
segments.s269-g225: Structure: از + NP + infinitive + 'امید نیست' expresses lack of hope 'there is no hope of ...'. Here از این کینه ('of/with regard to this grudge') is the thing concerning which hope is denied; برگشتن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'return/turning (back)', and امید نیست is the copula negating existence of that hope. Word order is typical Persian: preposition phrase → verb-noun → predicate.
segments.s269-g226: شب و روز is a coordinated subject ('night and day'); بی‌دیدگان را uses را as an object/topic marker marking the affected people ('the sightless' or 'those without sight'), and یکیست (archaic fused form of یکی است) is a verblike copula meaning 'are one / are the same'. The construction literally says 'night and day for the sightless are one.'
segments.s269-g227: چو means 'when' introducing a temporal clause; آن پاسخ‌نامه گشت = 'that reply/answer was made/turned into being' with گشت used in the sense 'became / was formed'. The word اسپری does not match the modern senses listed and is likely an archaic/proper or poetic term (probably referring to a mounted rider or horse) rather than the modern 'spray'.
segments.s269-g228: فرستاده is the noun 'messenger' (literally 'the sent one') serving as the subject; آمد is simple past 'came'. به سان is a fixed simile marker 'in the manner of / like' and پری is the simile's head noun 'a fairy' (poetic image). Note that به + سان functions together as 'like'.
segments.s269-g229: کمر بر میان is an idiomatic phrase 'gird the loins / prepare oneself' with کمر as the object and بر میان indicating the place/position 'at the middle' (i.e. about the waist). با ستور 'with steeds' marks accompaniment or means. نوند is an archaic/poetic term here (not matched by the modern senses given) and probably modifies or names the horses or a manner—treated as a lexical item whose precise modern gloss is uncertain.
segments.s269-g230: ز مردان 'of/from the men' introduces the group; به گرد اندرش = 'around him / about him' (به گرد = around; اندر + ش = 'in/at + his' → 'at him / around him'); نیز چند 'also a few' gives the quantifier. The overall syntax: 'Of the men, a few also gathered around him.'
segments.s269-g231: فرود آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'he alighted / descended'. از باره literally 'from the bāreh' (بارِه) uses باره in the sense 'load / burden / pack', so از باره indicates dismounting or getting off the load; رویین گرد is a compound adjective 'iron-clad / iron-circled' modifying that load or equipment. The phrase describes dismounting from an iron-armored mount or saddle.
segments.s269-g232: گوان را همه پیش گودرز برد has the pattern [object را] [destination] [past verb]: 'he took/brought all the گوان before Goudarz.' را marks the definite object; پیش گودرز 'in the presence of / before Goudarz' is the goal/recipient phrase. Note: گوان and برد do not match the modern senses provided (گوان is likely a name or archaic noun and برد here is the past of بردن 'to bring/take'), so those entries are marked unavailable in the supplied sense list.
segments.s269-g177: به هشتم is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning “regarding the eighth (one).” که introduces reported speech/relative content (“when/that”), and گفتی is a past 2nd-person verb “you said.” The sequence م + را is the object-clitic ‘me’ (classical/poetic splitting of مرا into م + را) where را is the definite-object marker; تاج و تخت are the direct objects (“crown and throne”).
segments.s269-g178: از آن here introduces a point of comparison or reference (“in that / from that”), تو is the addressed subject “you,” and بیشست is an archaic/poetic verbal/adjectival form meaning essentially “were more / surpassed” (comparative sense). مردی و بخت are coordinated nouns (“manliness/valor and fortune”) that بیشست applies to.
segments.s269-g179: همیدون is a proper name (a rival or other person). فزونم is a predicative form with first-person ending (-م) meaning “I am more / I have more” or “I exceed”; به introduces the standard of comparison (“in/with respect to”), and مردان و گنج are the things in which the speaker is greater (men/retainers and treasure).
segments.s269-g180: ولیکن is a discursive adversative conjunction “but / yet.” دلم را is the possessive-object phrase “my heart” + object marker را. The clause ز مهرست رنج literally arranges cause and predicate: ز introduces cause “from / because of,” مهرست is a fused poetic form (مهر + است) meaning “is love/affection,” and رنج is the noun “suffering/torment” — together: “my heart is tormented from love.”
segments.s269-g181: من is the emphatic subject “I.” ایدون is an adverbial/classical expression meaning ‘hitherto / up to now’ (poetic), and گمانم is the speaker’s belief/predicate “my thought / I supposed.” که is the subordinating particle “that,” and تا این زمان repeats the temporal sense “until this time” (reinforcing ‘hitherto’).
segments.s269-g182: به جنگ is the adverbial phrase “in battle/in war.” آزمودی is the past 2nd‑person verbal form “you tested / you tried,” and the following م + را again represents the object-clitic ‘me’ (classical splitting of مرا). بی‌گمان is an adverb “undoubtedly/without doubt,” placed to qualify the clause.
segments.s269-g183: گرم here marks a protasis (classical/poetic conditional: گر + enclitic) and introduces the hypothetical “if (you) …”; بی‌هنر is the adjective “unskilled / unversed,” and یافتی is a past-form verb “you are found / you are found to be” (literally ‘you found’ but functionally passive/experiential). روز کین is a genitival phrase “the day of vengeance” (روز = day; کین = vengeance/grudge) specifying when the test occurs.
segments.s269-g184: تو دانی is an independent clause “you know” (present), کنون means “now.” بازم is used adverbially/temporal here (sense ‘again / in return / henceforth’ in context) — its listed senses don’t match exactly so treated as an archaic adverb — and از پس means “afterwards / thereafter.” ببین is the 2nd‑person singular imperative “see / observe” (here: “you will see what comes after”).
segments.s269-g257: به نزدیک = a prepositional phrase meaning “near / close to”; به here indicates motion/toward or proximity and نزدیک is the adverb/adjective that follows it. تختش is ‘throne + possessive suffix (his)’. فرو برد is a compound verbal idea: فرو (down) + برد (past of بردن ‘to bring/take’); together فروبردن = “to lower / bow (one’s) head,” with سر (head) as the object. Word order is prepositional phrase → verb (compound) → object, a poetic ordering of elements.
segments.s269-g258: جهاندیده پیران is an epithet + proper name: ‘the world‑wise Piran’ (جهاندیده qualifies پیران). گرفتش is گرفت + enclitic ـش (object ‘him’): past tense ‘took/seized him’. به بر is an idiom meaning ‘into (his) bosom/arms’ (به = into/toward, بر = on/upon → here ‘to the breast/embrace’). The clause reads: ‘the world‑wise Piran took him to his arms.’
segments.s269-g259: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. بگزارد is an archaic/poetic third‑person form of گزار‌دن ‘to place/deliver’ (here ‘delivered/placed’). پیغام is the direct object ‘message’. سالار شاه is a genitive juxtaposition (no ezafe) meaning ‘the commander of the king’ (i.e. the king’s commander). The whole unit: ‘When the king’s commander delivered the message…’.
segments.s269-g260: بگفت is the past reporting verb ‘he said / he reported’. آنچ = contraction of آن‌چیزِ (literally ‘that which’ = ‘what’). دید is past ‘saw’ and functions as the verb inside the content clause: ‘that which he saw’. اندر آن رزمگاه is the locative phrase ‘in/on that battlefield’. Word order: reporting verb → object clause introduced by آنچ → subordinate clause with its verb (دید) → locative.
segments.s269-g261: پس = ‘then/after’. آن نامه = ‘that letter’ (definite object). برخواند here means ‘read aloud’ (برخواندن), a perfective/past action performed by the agent. پیشش دبیر = ‘the scribe in his presence’ or ‘the scribe before him’: دبیر is the agent (subject) and پیشش is an adverbial ‘in front of him’ — in epic prose the agent can follow the adverbial, producing this order: (temporal) → object → verb → adverbial → subject.
segments.s269-g262: رخ پهلوان = ‘the face/countenance of the hero’ (noun + epithet). شد is the past of شدن ‘became’. چو قیر = a simile ‘like tar’. سپه here functions as a predicate element with شد (poetic/archaic usage—an adjectival/qualitative word applied to رخ); the clause means ‘the hero’s face became (as) tar’ i.e. turned dark/ashen/black as tar. Note: سپه is a poetic/archaised lexical item used predicatively with شد.
segments.s269-g263: دلش = ‘his heart’ (دل + possessive ـش). گشت (past of گشتن here) = ‘became / turned’. پردرد = compound adjective ‘full of pain / sorrowful’ (پر + درد). و links the two coordinated phrases. جان پرنهیب = ‘his life/soul full of awe/terror’ — پرنهیب is a poetic adjective meaning ‘full of awe/dread’ (پر = full of + نهیب). The structure: subject (implied actor’s) heart became… and his life became…
segments.s269-g264: بدانست = ‘he knew/realized it’ (past). کآمد is an archaic fused connector + verb (ک + آمد) meaning ‘that (it) came’ or ‘what came’; it introduces the content of his knowledge. به تنگی = ‘into narrowness / distress’ (به = into/toward, تنگی = hardship/oppression). نشیب = ‘a descent / low point’ — together this phrase means he realized that a fall into hardship had arrived. The clause uses older poetic morphology (کآمد) to link the cognition verb to its content clause.
segments.s269-g169: به ... گفتار نیست = a topical/prepositional frame meaning 'as for / regarding ... there is no speech'. Here the phrase 'به پیمان مرا با تو' functions as 'regarding my covenant with you' (پیمان + possessive م[را] + با تو). گفتار نیست is a verbless or copular negative: گفتار ('speech') is the thing that 'is not' (نیست) — idiomatically 'no need of words' or 'words are unnecessary'.
